planting.note: Divide rhizomes or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial to full shade in spring; rodgersia requires reliably moist to boggy soil and will not perform well if allowed to dry out — a mulched site near a stream, pond, or rain garden is ideal.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial to full shade; featherleaf rodgersia (elder-leafed rodgersia) is a Chinese shade perennial of exceptional character, producing some of the most bold and handsome foliage of any shade garden plant — the large, pinnately divided leaves with five to nine leaflets resemble an oversized elderberry leaf and are arranged in striking rosettes that expand from reddish-bronze emerging buds in spring to large, deep-green, prominently veined, leathery-textured plates that color to rich bronze, copper, and burgundy tones in autumn. In early to mid-summer the plant sends up tall, branching, astilbe-like flower plumes of tiny, pink to creamy-white, starry flowers well above the foliage. The cultivar 'Superba' with deep-pink flowers and bronze-flushed foliage is among the most widely grown, and 'Chocolate Wing' (dark-chocolate-red spring and fall foliage) is exceptional. Rodgersia requires consistently moist to wet soil and dislikes drought — it is ideal beside water features and in wet shade gardens where it can eventually colonize large areas with its slowly spreading rhizomes.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; dwarf goat's beard is considerably more tolerant of dry shade than the larger Aruncus dioicus and performs acceptably in drier woodland conditions where the larger species would decline.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in moist, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; dwarf goat's beard is a diminutive Korean species that provides all the ornamental qualities of the full-sized goat's beard (Aruncus dioicus) in a compact, groundcover-scale package suitable for smaller gardens and shaded edging — the finely divided, fernlike, bright-green, delicate leaves form a tidy, low, cushion-shaped mound, and the plant produces small but charming, feathery, white flower plumes in late spring to early summer. The foliage is among the finest-textured of any shade perennial and creates a delicate, lacey effect in the shade border. After the flowers fade, the seed heads turn russet-brown and remain ornamental through late summer. In autumn the leaves develop attractive yellow-orange coloring. A refined, tidy, and well-behaved shade perennial — it does not self-seed aggressively and remains in a controlled clump. Excellent as a low groundcover under deciduous trees, in rock garden shaded pockets, and at the front of shaded borders. More drought-tolerant than the species once established.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant tubers 3–4 inches deep in moist, strongly acidic to slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in early spring as soil becomes workable; Arisaema sikokianum is considered the most ornamental jack-in-the-pulpit species and is prized by woodland garden collectors — the combination of the dark-purple-and-white striped spathe with the brilliant white clubbed spadix is unmatched in the genus.

directions: Plant tubers in moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Japanese jack-in-the-pulpit is widely regarded as the most spectacular and sought-after member of the jack-in-the-pulpit genus (Arisaema) — in late spring, each tuber produces a single, dramatically beautiful flower structure in which a dark-chocolate-purple to deep-maroon spathe (the hooded 'pulpit'), boldly striped on the inside with white, arches gracefully over a pure, glistening, snow-white, clubbed spadix (the 'jack') — the contrast of the dark, dramatically streaked hood against the brilliant white club is exceptionally striking and completely unlike the green-striped spathes of the common North American Jack-in-the-pulpit. The two or three large leaves, with five leaflets each, emerge simultaneously and are themselves attractive. The plant goes completely dormant by midsummer. Like all Arisaema species, it requires cool, moist, acidic, woodland soil conditions typical of a Japanese woodland garden. A prized collector's plant for the serious shade gardener — more ornamental in flower than almost any other temperate shade perennial.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich soil in partial shade in spring; pink turtlehead is one of the best late-blooming shade perennials for wet conditions — it performs at its best beside streams, ponds, and in rain gardens where the soil remains consistently moist.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in consistently moist to wet, average to humus-rich soil in partial shade; pink turtlehead is a southeastern Appalachian native shade and moisture perennial of the family Plantaginaceae that produces upright spikes of inflated, two-lipped, deep-rose-pink flowers in late summer and early fall (August–October) — the unique, puffy, snapdragon-like flowers have a distinctly turtle-headed shape (the two lips nearly closed) that gives the plant its charming common name, and are visited preferentially by bumblebees large enough to force apart the lips and access the nectar. It blooms at the same time as asters and late sedums, extending the partial-shade flowering season into autumn. The plant thrives in the wet, partially shaded conditions of streambanks, pond edges, and moist woodland gardens where it spreads gradually by rhizomes to form attractive colonies. 'Hot Lips' (particularly vivid rose-pink with a white throat) and 'Tiny Tortuga' (more compact, 18-inch form) are excellent cultivars. An important nectar plant for migrating monarch butterflies and late-season native bees.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide clumps carefully in early spring or sow fresh seed in fall, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade in spring; lavender mist meadowrue resents root disturbance and is best left undisturbed once established — it is slower to establish than many perennials but becomes a long-lived and increasingly beautiful garden plant over time.

directions: Divide clumps carefully or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; lavender mist meadowrue is a Japanese tall shade perennial that creates one of the most ethereal and romantic effects in the summer shade garden — rising to 5–7 feet on slender, dark, metallic-purple-blue stems with large, delicate, blue-green, columbine-like, compound leaves, then erupting in mid-summer into enormous, airy clouds of tiny, lavender-lilac to rosy-purple, pendant flowers with long, showy, yellow-tipped stamens that create a hovering, transparent, lavender mist above the foliage. The combination of the dark, metallic stems, the blue-green ferny foliage, and the airy lavender flower cloud creates one of the finest and most distinctive effects available in the shade garden. It is considerably more impressive in stature than the European species (Thalictrum aquilegiifolium) and blooms later, in July–August. It needs staking only in windy or very shaded positions. The dark stem color is a distinctive identification feature. An outstanding background plant for large shade borders.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
